content:
**Executive summary:** Matter is anything with mass and volume. The **molecule** is the smallest independent unit showing all properties of a substance; molecules of one pure substance are identical. **Cohesion** (same substance) and **adhesion** (different substances) are intermolecular attractions that weaken with distance — they explain why water drops merge and glue sticks paper.

**Must know**
• **Matter** = has mass + occupies space (iron, water, air, wood, oil, steam).
• **Element** → smallest unit = **atom**; cannot be broken into simpler substances chemically.
• **Molecule** = smallest unit existing independently with all properties of that matter; may be one or more atoms (NaCl = 1 Na + 1 Cl).
• Molecules of a given substance: **identical** in shape, size, mass.
• Molecules in **continuous random motion** → possess kinetic energy (never fully at rest).
• **Cohesion**: attraction between **same** substance molecules (water–water; merging drops).
• **Adhesion**: attraction between **different** substance molecules (glue–paper, water–glass, ink–paper).
• Cohesion strength varies: chalk (weak) < brick < iron (very strong).
• Ice, water, steam = same **H₂O molecules**; only arrangement, spacing and energy differ (physical change).

**Molecular properties**
Molecules attract via **intermolecular forces** — these weaken rapidly as distance increases. **Cohesion** pulls like molecules together; **adhesion** binds unlike surfaces. Chalk powders easily because cohesion is weak; iron is hard to break because cohesion is very strong.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Cohesion vs Adhesion**
| Criterion | Cohesion | Adhesion |
|---|---|---|
| Acts between | Molecules of the **same** substance | Molecules of **different** substances |
| Example | Two water drops merging | Glue sticking to paper |
| Everyday test | Drop wets itself (merges) | Liquid wets a surface (sticks) |
| Exam trap | Often swapped in T/F questions | Glue sticking = adhesion, not cohesion |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the ICSE definition of matter?
• How is a molecule different from an atom?
• Which force makes two water drops merge — cohesion or adhesion?
• Why is chalk easier to powder than iron?
• Are ice and steam molecules chemically different from water molecules?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Cohesion ≠ adhesion — examiners deliberately swap them in true/false.
• Statement "cohesion acts between different substances" is **false** — that is adhesion.
• "Liquid wax melts" is wrong wording — solid wax melts at ~55 °C to become liquid.
• Molecules of **different** substances differ in size and mass — they are NOT identical across substances.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s1-sec_02 — intermolecular forces and spacing determine the three states; phy-s1-sec_03 — kinetic theory builds on molecular motion and attraction; phy-s1-sec_04 — latent heat overcomes these forces during change of state.

• Cohesion is the attraction between molecules of the same substance.
• Adhesion is the force of attraction between molecules of different substances.
• I is correct — molecules of the same substance are identical.
• Matter is defined as anything that has mass and occupies space — the fundamental ICSE definition.
• T/F: "The force of adhesion acts between the molecules of the same substance." → **false** — False.
• Fill: **cohesion** — Cohesion — e.

content:
**Executive summary:** Matter exists as **solid, liquid and gas** — differences arise from **intermolecular force** and **intermolecular space**. Solids are closely packed and incompressible; liquids have definite volume but no fixed shape; gases fill any closed vessel and are highly compressible. Remember three order chains: force (solid > liquid > gas), space (gas > liquid > solid), compressibility (gas > liquid > solid).

**Must know**
• **Solid**: negligible spaces; strongest forces; molecules **vibrate about fixed positions** → definite shape + definite volume; practically incompressible.
• **Liquid**: slightly larger spaces; weaker forces; molecules **slide past one another** → definite volume, no definite shape; **definite free surface**; slightly compressible.
• **Gas**: very large spaces; negligible forces; molecules move **freely at high speed** → no definite shape or volume; **no free surface**; highly compressible; needs closed vessel.
• Compression works by reducing **empty space** between molecules — almost none left in solids.
• On melting: spaces **increase**, forces **weaken** (molecules intact — physical change).
• Intermolecular force order: **gas < liquid < solid** (increasing).
• Intermolecular space order: **solid < liquid < gas** (increasing).

**Why gases fill vessels**
With negligible intermolecular forces, gas molecules fly freely in all directions and spread until they occupy the **entire vessel** — neither definite shape nor volume. A gas has **no free surface**; only liquids have one.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Solid vs Liquid vs Gas**
| Property | Solid | Liquid | Gas |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular motion | Vibrate at fixed positions | Slide past one another | Move freely, high speed |
| Shape | Definite | Takes container shape | Fills container |
| Volume | Definite | Definite | Not definite |
| Free surface | No (rigid) | **Yes** | **No** |
| Compressibility | Almost none | Slight | Very high |
| Intermolecular force | Strongest | Moderate | Negligible |
| Intermolecular space | Smallest | Larger than solid | Largest |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Why can a gas be compressed but not a solid?
• Does a liquid have a definite shape? A definite volume? A free surface?
• Arrange solid, liquid, gas in increasing intermolecular force.
• Why does a gas completely fill a closed vessel?
• What two molecular changes occur when a solid melts?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Liquid spaces are **larger** than solid spaces — NOT smaller (common T/F trap).
• Solids are incompressible because spaces are negligible — NOT because molecules repel at all distances.
• Gases compress easily; liquids compress only slightly — reversing this is a classic Olympiad trap.
• A liquid has definite **volume** only — never definite shape.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s1-sec_01 — cohesion/adhesion are the forces behind these states; phy-s1-sec_03 — kinetic theory explains WHY each state behaves this way; phy-s1-sec_04 — heating supplies energy to overcome forces and increase spacing.

**From linked questions (12 Qs):**
• Compression works by reducing the empty space between molecules.
• Liquid molecules remain bound to each other (hence definite volume) but the forces are weaker than in solids, so molecules can slide over one another — letting the liquid flow and take the container's shape.
• Intermolecular forces are almost negligible in gases, moderate in liquids and strongest in solids.
• With almost no attractive forces to hold them together, gas molecules fly about freely at high speeds and spread to every part of the vessel.
• A is true — solids are nearly incompressible.
• Closely packed molecules with negligible intermolecular spaces and fixed positions characterise a SOLID.
• In a liquid the intermolecular forces are weaker than in a solid — strong enough to hold the molecules together (definite volume) but weak enough to let them slide over one another.
• T/F: "Intermolecular spaces in a liquid are smaller than those in a solid." → **false** — False.

content:
**Executive summary:** The **kinetic theory** explains all states of matter through five assumptions: molecules exist, move randomly, gain kinetic energy with temperature, attract each other, and have empty spaces between them. It accounts for diffusion, gas pressure, fixed solid shape, and why steam at 100 °C burns worse than boiling water.

**Must know**
• Matter = tiny **molecules**; molecules of one substance are **identical**.
• **Continuous random motion** — never stops; molecules possess **kinetic energy**.
• **Average kinetic energy ∝ temperature** — heating speeds molecules up; molecules do NOT grow or multiply.
• Molecules **attract** (cohesion/adhesion); attraction weakens with distance.
• **Intermolecular spaces** exist — smallest in solids, largest in gases.
• **Solid shape**: molecules vibrate but cannot leave fixed positions.
• **Diffusion**: perfume/bromine molecules wander through air spaces randomly — even denser gases mix upward against gravity (dynamic equilibrium, motion never stops).
• **Gas pressure**: billions of molecular **collisions per second** on walls → steady outward push in all directions.
• **Energy order** (same substance): steam > water > ice.
• Solid molecules **never stop** — they vibrate about fixed mean positions.

**Theory in action**
Raising temperature increases **average kinetic energy** only — not molecular size, number, or intermolecular force strength. Gas molecules rebound from walls; each collision exerts a tiny push — the sum is **pressure**.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — What Kinetic Theory Explains**
| Observation | Kinetic theory explanation |
|---|---|
| Perfume spreads in a room | Random molecular motion through air spaces (diffusion) |
| Gas fills entire vessel | Negligible forces → free motion in all directions |
| Solid keeps definite shape | Strong forces → vibration about fixed positions only |
| Gas exerts wall pressure | Continuous high-speed collisions + rebounds |
| Steam burns worse than hot water | Steam carries extra latent heat released on condensing |
| Bromine mixes upward in air | Random KE overcomes gravity; dynamic equilibrium |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• State the five main assumptions of kinetic theory.
• Why do solid molecules NOT stop moving at 0 °C?
• What does temperature actually measure at the molecular level?
• How does kinetic theory explain gas pressure?
• Why does perfume reach the far corner of a closed room?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• "Molecules stop in a solid" is **false** — they vibrate in place.
• Uniform colour in diffusion = **dynamic equilibrium**, NOT stopped motion.
• Lower temperature **slows** diffusion — it does not speed it up.
• Steam and boiling water at 100 °C have the **same average KE** — steam's extra danger is **latent heat of vaporisation**.
• Any 'explain using kinetic theory' answer needs **BOTH** intermolecular force AND space.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s1-sec_01 — molecular identity and intermolecular forces; phy-s1-sec_02 — the three states are kinetic-theory snapshots; phy-s1-sec_04 — latent heat changes PE (separation) while KE stays constant during change of state.

**From linked questions (9 Qs):**
• Gas molecules move randomly at high speed.
• Both are at 100 °C, so the average kinetic energy of molecules is the same.
• Solid molecules are never completely at rest — they constantly vibrate, but strong intermolecular forces keep each molecule oscillating about a fixed position.
• Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of molecules.
• Gas molecules move randomly at high speeds and keep striking the container walls.
• According to the Kinetic Theory, gas particles possess high kinetic energy and move randomly.
• T/F: "The molecules of a solid are completely at rest." → **false** — False.
• T/F: "On heating a substance, the average kinetic energy of its molecules increases." → **true** — True.

subtopic: 4. Change of State, Evaporation and Boiling
content:
**Executive summary:** A **change of state** is a physical change — molecules stay the same; only spacing, forces and energy change. During melting/boiling, **latent heat** is absorbed without temperature rise. **Evaporation** (surface, all temperatures, cooling) differs from **boiling** (bulk, fixed temperature). Evaporation rate rises with surface area, temperature, wind and low humidity — NOT with depth of liquid.

**Must know**
• **Melting (fusion)**: solid → liquid at fixed **melting point** (pure ice: 0 °C); temperature constant.
• **Vaporisation**: liquid → gas (evaporation or boiling).
• **Condensation**: gas → liquid; **releases** latent heat (dew, droplets on cold glass/bottle).
• **Freezing**: liquid → solid (reverse of melting).
• **Sublimation**: solid → vapour directly (camphor, iodine, naphthalene).
• **Latent heat**: supplied heat overcomes intermolecular forces — does NOT increase molecular speed during change of state.
• During sublimation at constant T: **KE constant**, **PE increases** (molecules separate).
• **Evaporation**: ALL temperatures below boiling point; **surface only**; slow; causes **cooling**.
• **Boiling**: ONE fixed temperature (water 100 °C); **throughout bulk**; rapid; temperature stays constant.
• Evaporation cooling: most energetic molecules escape → average KE of remainder **falls**.
• **Steam at 100 °C** burns worse than boiling water — carries latent heat of vaporisation released on condensing.
• **Ice at 0 °C** cools better than water at 0 °C — absorbs latent heat of fusion while melting.

**Applications**
Matka (earthen pot): water seeps through pores → evaporates → latent heat drawn from inside water. Sweating, sanitizer, sprinkled desert water, cotton clothes — all use **evaporation + latent heat** cooling.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Evaporation vs Boiling**
| Criterion | Evaporation | Boiling |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Any temp below boiling point | Fixed boiling point only |
| Where it occurs | **Surface** only | **Throughout bulk** (bubbles) |
| Speed | Slow | Rapid |
| Effect on liquid temp | **Cools** remaining liquid | Stays at boiling point (heat supplied externally) |
| Energy source for escaping molecules | Taken from liquid itself | Supplied by external heater |
| Depth of liquid matters? | **No** — surface phenomenon | No |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Why does temperature stay at 0 °C while ice melts?
• What is sublimation? Name three subliming substances.
• Why does evaporation cool but boiling does not lower liquid temperature?
• Which four factors increase evaporation rate?
• Why is steam at 100 °C more dangerous than boiling water at 100 °C?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Pouring more water into a dish (deeper, same surface area) does NOT increase evaporation rate.
• During boiling, heat does NOT increase molecular speed — it supplies **latent heat of vaporisation**.
• Condensation **releases** heat; vaporisation **absorbs** heat.
• Examples of sublimation (camphor, iodine) do NOT explain what sublimation is — they are illustrations only.
• Evaporation **lowers** temperature of remaining liquid — statement III in statement-based questions is often wrong.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s1-sec_02 — change of state alters spacing and forces between states; phy-s1-sec_03 — latent heat changes PE while average KE (temperature) stays constant; phy-s1-sec_01 — overcoming intermolecular forces requires latent heat.

**From linked questions (27 Qs):**
• Camphor changes directly from solid to vapour without passing through the liquid state — this is sublimation.
• During a change of state, the heat supplied (latent heat) does not raise the temperature.
• Evaporation increases with higher temperature (molecules gain kinetic energy), lower humidity (air can hold more vapour) and wind (moving air carries vapour molecules away from the surface).
• An earthen pot has tiny pores through which water continuously seeps out and evaporates from the outer surface.
• On melting, molecules gain energy, vibrate more violently and break away from fixed positions.
• Evaporation is a surface phenomenon — it depends on the exposed surface area, temperature, wind and humidity, not on the depth or total amount of liquid.
• Water vapour present in air loses energy on touching the cold outer surface of the glass; its molecules slow down, come closer, and the vapour condenses into liquid droplets.
• Sanitizer (alcohol) is a volatile liquid that evaporates quickly.

examTip: For 'give reason' cooling questions (matka, sweating, sanitizer), always name BOTH evaporation AND latent heat drawn from the body/liquid — each earns a mark.

content:
**Executive summary:** **Density** = mass ÷ volume — mass packed per unit volume. SI unit **kg/m³**, CGS **g/cm³**; **1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³**. Water at 4 °C = 1 g/cm³. Density is a **characteristic property** — same for any size piece of a pure substance. **Relative density** = density of substance ÷ density of water — a pure ratio with **no unit**.

**Must know**
• Density = mass ÷ volume; SI: **kg/m³**; CGS: **g/cm³**.
• Conversion: **1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³** (multiply g/cm³ by 1000).
• Water density = 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ (at 4 °C) — reference for R.D.
• **Characteristic property**: small and large pieces of same pure substance → **same density**.
• On heating: mass unchanged, volume **expands** → density **decreases**.
• **R.D.** = density of substance ÷ density of water; **no unit** (units cancel).
• R.D. of iron ≈ 7.8 → iron is 7.8× denser than water.
• Equal volumes: denser substance is **heavier**. Equal masses: less dense substance occupies **more volume**.

**Approximate R.D. values**
cork 0.25 | wood 0.6–0.8 | kerosene 0.8 | ice 0.9 | water 1.0 | milk 1.03 | sea water 1.025 | aluminium 2.7 | iron/steel 7.8 | mercury 13.6 | honey ~1.4
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Density vs Relative Density**
| Criterion | Density | Relative Density (R.D.) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | mass ÷ volume | density of substance ÷ density of water |
| SI unit | kg/m³ | **None** (pure ratio) |
| CGS unit | g/cm³ | **None** |
| Water value | 1000 kg/m³ or 1 g/cm³ | Exactly **1.0** |
| Conversion | 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ | R.D. × 1000 = density in kg/m³ |
| Use | Identify substance; float/sink | Quick comparison with water |
| Changes on heating? | Decreases (expansion) | Decreases similarly |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What makes density a characteristic property?
• Convert 2.7 g/cm³ to SI units.
• Why does relative density have no unit?
• What happens to density when a liquid is heated?
• If R.D. = 0.8, what is the density in kg/m³?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• R.D. is NOT measured in g/cm³ or kg/m³ — it is unitless.
• R.D. of mercury 13.6 means 13.6× denser than water — R correctly restates A.
• Heating decreases density — mass stays same, volume grows.
• Compare **densities**, never masses alone, to decide float/sink.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s2-sec_02 — laboratory measurement of density; phy-s2-sec_03 — floatation depends on comparing densities; phy-s2-sec_09 — numerical conversions and R.D. calculations.

**From linked questions (17 Qs):**
• To convert g/cm³ to kg/m³, multiply by 1000 (because 1 g = 10⁻³ kg and 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³, so 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³).
• Relative density = density of substance ÷ density of water.
• Heating makes the liquid expand: the same mass now occupies a larger volume, so density (mass ÷ volume) decreases.
• For equal volumes, the body with more mass packed into that volume has the higher density.
• For a regular solid, the volume is best calculated from its dimensions (l × b × h measured with a scale), and the mass from a beam balance; then density = mass/volume.
• Relative density is, by definition, the number of times a substance is denser than water.
• Doubling the piece doubles both mass and volume, so their ratio (density) is unchanged.
• I and III are correct.

content:
**Executive summary:** Density needs **mass** (beam balance) and **volume**. Regular solids: V = l × b × h. Irregular sinking solids: **displacement method** (eureka can or measuring cylinder). Floating solids (cork, wax): tie to a **sinker** — V_solid = V_(sinker+solid) − V_sinker. Then density = mass ÷ volume.

**Must know**
• **Regular solid** (cube, cuboid, cylinder): measure dimensions with metre scale → V = l × b × h.
• **Irregular solid that sinks**: fully submerge → displaces water equal to its own volume.
  – Measuring cylinder: V = final reading − initial reading.
  – **Eureka (displacement) can**: fill to spout, lower solid on thread, collect overflow.
• **Irregular solid lighter than water** (cork, wax): attach **sinker** (heavy metal piece).
  – Submerge sinker alone → reading V₁.
  – Submerge sinker + solid → reading V₂.
  – Volume of solid = **V₂ − V₁**.
• Never push floating solid with finger — finger volume adds to reading.
• **Precautions**: fully submerged; lower gently (no splashing); read lower meniscus at eye level; thread volume negligible.
• Order of experiment: **mass first**, then volume, then ρ = m/V.

**Instruments**
Beam balance → mass. Eureka can + measuring cylinder → volume of irregular solids. Measuring cylinder alone → liquid volume or displacement rise.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Volume Measurement Methods**
| Type of body | Method | Volume formula |
|---|---|---|
| Regular solid (block) | Metre scale dimensions | V = l × b × h |
| Irregular, sinks (stone) | Displacement in cylinder or eureka can | V = V₂ − V₁ |
| Irregular, floats (cork) | Sinker method | V = V_(sinker+cork) − V_sinker |
| Liquid | Measuring cylinder | Direct reading (mL = cm³) |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What two measurements are needed for density?
• How do you find volume of a stone using a eureka can?
• Why is a sinker needed for cork?
• Why should you never push a floating object down with your finger?
• What is the correct order of steps in a density experiment?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Eureka can measures **volume**, not mass or density directly.
• Rise in water level = volume of **fully submerged** solid (not partial).
• For a wooden block, use dimensions — eureka can is for **irregular** solids only.
• 1 mL = 1 cm³ — use consistently with grams.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s2-sec_01 — density formula and units; phy-s2-sec_09 — numerical worked examples using displacement; phy-s2-sec_03 — knowing density lets you predict float/sink.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• When a solid is lowered into a eureka can filled up to its spout, it displaces water equal to its own VOLUME, which is collected and read in a measuring cylinder.
• A solid lighter than water will not submerge on its own.
• T/F: "When a stone is fully submerged in a measuring cylinder, the rise in water level equals the volume of the stone." → **true** — True.
• 1.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• When a solid is lowered into a eureka can filled up to its spout, it displaces water equal to its own VOLUME, which is collected and read in a measuring cylinder.
• A solid lighter than water will not submerge on its own.
• T/F: "When a stone is fully submerged in a measuring cylinder, the rise in water level equals the volume of the stone." → **true** — True.
• 1.
teacherTip: **S**inker for **S**wimmers (floaters). **D**isplacement for **D**ivers (sinkers). Mass first, volume second, divide last — **MVD**.
examTip: For 'describe an experiment' answers, write steps in order: mass → volume by displacement → formula. The sinker twist is the favourite examiner surprise.

content:
**Executive summary:** A body **floats** if its density ≤ liquid density; **sinks** if greater. A floating body displaces liquid whose **weight equals its own weight**. Applications: icebergs (~9/10 submerged), steel ships (hollow hull lowers average density), submarines (ballast tanks), hydrometer/lactometer (floatation principle).

**Must know**
• **Float**: density of body ≤ density of liquid.
• **Sink**: density of body > density of liquid.
• **Law of floatation**: floating body displaces liquid of **weight = body's weight**.
• **Ice/iceberg**: R.D. ≈ 0.9 → ~**9/10 submerged**, ~1/10 visible — danger to ships.
• **Steel ship**: hollow hull encloses air → **average density** (steel + air) < water — despite steel sinking alone.
• **Submarine**: flood ballast tanks with sea water → average density rises → dive; blow water out with compressed air → rise.
• **Egg in brine**: salt raises water density above egg's density → floats.
• **Sea vs river**: denser sea water → greater upthrust → easier swimming.
• **Oil/kerosene** (R.D. ≈ 0.8) floats on water — lower density, immiscible.
• **Hydrometer**: weighted float; sinks **deeper in less dense** liquid; scale increases **downward** on stem.
• **Lactometer**: hydrometer for milk; added water lowers density → sinks deeper (adulteration test).
• **Helium balloon** rises (less dense than air); breath-filled balloon sinks (slightly denser with rubber).
• Floats in A, sinks in B → density of A ≥ body > density of B.

**Hydrometer design**
Heavy bulb keeps it upright; thin stem gives large readable depth change for small density differences. No weighing needed — read at liquid surface.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Float vs Sink Decision Logic**
| Situation | Density comparison | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Cork in water | cork < water | Floats (upthrust > weight) |
| Iron ball in water | iron > water | Sinks |
| Steel ship | average(steel+air) < water | Floats |
| Iceberg | ice ≈ 0.9 × water | Floats, 9/10 submerged |
| Egg in fresh water | egg > water | Sinks |
| Egg in salt water | egg < salt water | Floats |
| Hydrometer in alcohol vs water | alcohol less dense | Deeper in alcohol |
| Wax in water vs alcohol | wax between 0.8 and 1.0 | Floats in water, sinks in alcohol |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• State the law of floatation in one sentence.
• Why does a steel ship float but a steel ball sinks?
• Why is only 1/10 of an iceberg visible?
• How does a lactometer detect watered milk?
• A body floats in liquid A but sinks in B — what can you conclude?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Steel is denser than water — ships float due to **average density**, not because steel is light.
• A floating body is usually **partly submerged** — not wholly above the surface.
• Hydrometer sinks deeper in **less dense** liquid — do not reverse.
• Hydrometer measures **liquids**, not solids.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s2-sec_01 — density and R.D. values drive float/sink; phy-s2-sec_02 — measuring density of irregular bodies; phy-s2-sec_09 — numerical density comparisons for float/sink.

**From linked questions (28 Qs):**
• A body floats on a liquid if its density is less than that of the liquid.
• Floating depends on AVERAGE density, not the density of the material alone.
• Floating in A means density of solid ≤ density of A.
• A floating hydrometer sinks until it displaces liquid whose weight equals its own weight.
• Cork is much less dense than water.
• Letting sea water into the ballast tanks increases the submarine's mass without changing its volume, so its average density rises above that of sea water and it sinks.
• Pure milk is denser than water (relative density ≈ 1.
• A floating body displaces liquid equal to its own weight.

teacherTip: **F**loat = **F**eather-light compared to liquid (density ≤ liquid). Ship = **S**hell of air inside steel. Hydrometer: **D**eeper = **D**ilute (less dense).
examTip: 'Floats in A, sinks in B' ⇒ density of A ≥ body > density of B. One line — practise until automatic.

content:
**Executive summary:** Master three rearrangements of **density = mass ÷ volume**. Keep units consistent: g with cm³, kg with m³. Use R.D. × 1000 for kg/m³. Always compare **densities** (not masses) for float/sink. Show formula → substitution → answer with unit.

**Must know**
• ρ = m/V | m = ρ × V | V = m/ρ
• 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³; 1 mL = 1 cm³
• R.D. → density in kg/m³: multiply R.D. by 1000
• Float/sink: compare body density with water (1 g/cm³)
• **Worked patterns**:
  – Displacement: 540 g, rise 60→260 cm³ → V=200 → ρ=540/200=**2.7 g/cm³** (aluminium)
  – Mass: V=25 cm³, ρ=8 g/cm³ → m=8×25=**200 g**
  – R.D.: 0.8 → ρ=0.8×1000=**800 kg/m³**
  – Float: 200 g, 250 cm³ → ρ=0.8 g/cm³ < 1 → **floats**

**Checklist**
Convert units FIRST, then substitute. State unit in every line — ICSE awards method marks.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Unit Consistency Guide**
| System | Mass | Volume | Density | Float reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CGS | g | cm³ (or mL) | g/cm³ | water = 1 g/cm³ |
| SI | kg | m³ | kg/m³ | water = 1000 kg/m³ |
| Bridge | — | 1 mL = 1 cm³ | ×1000 to convert g/cm³→kg/m³ | R.D. × 1000 = kg/m³ |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• A metal piece 540 g displaces 200 cm³ — find density and identify the metal.
• R.D. of a liquid is 0.8 — density in kg/m³?
• Block: 200 g, 250 cm³ — float or sink in water?
• Why must you never mix g with m³ in one calculation?
• If equal volumes of iron and wood are weighed, which is heavier and why?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Most errors = mixing unit systems mid-calculation — convert FIRST.
• Missing units loses a mark even with correct number.
• Whether a body floats depends on **density**, not mass alone.
• Dividing by a fraction (e.g. 400 N ÷ 0.5 m²) gives a **larger** answer — expect bigger pressure.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s2-sec_01 — definitions and R.D.; phy-s2-sec_02 — displacement gives volume for numericals; phy-s2-sec_03 — density comparison predicts float/sink outcomes.

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**
• Density = mass/volume = 200 g ÷ 250 cm³ = 0.
• Volume of metal = rise in water level = 260 − 60 = 200 cm³.
• Mass = density × volume = 8 g/cm³ × 25 cm³ = 200 g.
• Density of liquid = relative density × density of water = 0.

**From linked questions (4 Qs):**

• Volume of metal = rise in water level = 260 − 60 = 200 cm³.
• Mass = density × volume = 8 g/cm³ × 25 cm³ = 200 g.

subtopic: 1. Force: A Push or a Pull
content:
**Executive summary:** A **force** is a push or pull that can change rest/motion, speed, direction, or shape/size. SI unit: **newton (N)**. **Contact forces** (friction, muscular, normal reaction) need touching; **non-contact forces** (gravitational, magnetic, electrostatic) act at a distance. Force produces **translational** motion (bodily movement) or **rotational** motion (turning about an axis — studied as moment of force).

**Must know**
• Force = push or pull; can change: state of rest/motion, speed, direction, shape, size.
• SI unit: **newton (N)**.
• **Contact forces**: muscular force, friction, normal reaction — require physical contact.
• **Non-contact forces**: gravitational, magnetic, electrostatic — act at a distance.
• **Translational motion**: body moves bodily from one place to another.
• **Rotational (turning) motion**: body turns about axis/pivot (door on hinges, wheel on axle).
• Turning effect of force = **moment of force** (next section).
• Thrust and pressure deal with force spread over area (later section).

**Classification**
Every force you meet fits in **contact** or **non-contact** — with two examples of each ready for one-mark questions.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Contact vs Non-contact Forces**
| Criterion | Contact forces | Non-contact forces |
|---|---|---|
| Need touching? | **Yes** | **No** — act at distance |
| Examples | Friction, muscular, normal reaction | Gravitational, magnetic, electrostatic |
| Effect types | Push/pull, friction, support | Weight, magnetism, static charge |
| Chapter link | Moment, pressure on surfaces | Weight causes thrust/pressure |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What four changes can a force produce on a body?
• Give two contact and two non-contact force examples.
• What is the difference between translational and rotational motion?
• What is the SI unit of force?
• What quantity measures the turning effect of a force?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Force and thrust are related but not identical — thrust is force acting **normally** on a surface.
• Moment of force (N m) is NOT work (joule) — same dimensions, different physics.
• This chapter focuses on **moment** (turning) and **pressure** (force per area).

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s3-sec_02 — moment quantifies turning effect; phy-s3-sec_03 — thrust and pressure apply force over area; phy-s4-sec_01 — force × displacement = work (energy chapter).
examTip: Classify forces in two boxes — contact and non-contact — with two examples each; a one-mark classification appears almost every year.

subtopic: 2. Turning Effect of Force: Moment and Couple
content:
**Executive summary:** **Moment of force** = force × **perpendicular distance** from axis; unit **N m** (never joule). Larger distance → larger turning effect with same force. A **couple** = two equal, parallel, opposite forces at different points — net force zero but both moments act in the **same rotational direction**.

**Must know**
• **Moment = F × d** (d = perpendicular distance from axis; d in **metres**).
• SI unit: **newton metre (N m)** — written N m, NOT joule.
• Moment can be **clockwise** or **anticlockwise**.
• Force through the axis → perpendicular distance = 0 → **zero moment**.
• Small force, large effect: apply force **far from axis** (door handle at edge, long spanner, steering wheel radius, see-saw ends).
• **Couple**: two equal, parallel, **opposite** forces at different points.
  – Net force = 0, but both produce moments in **same sense** → rotation.
  – Examples: steering wheel, winding key, opening tap, screwdriver.
• Convert cm to m before calculating: 40 cm = 0.4 m.

**Applications**
Door handle at farthest point from hinges. Long spanner loosens tight nut. Force through hinge line cannot open door.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Moment vs Couple**
| Criterion | Moment of force | Couple |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | F × perpendicular distance | Two forces: equal, parallel, opposite |
| Net force | Can be non-zero | **Zero** |
| Turning effect | One force × distance | Both moments add in same direction |
| Zero moment when | Force passes through axis | Never — always rotates |
| Examples | Spanner, door push | Steering wheel, tap, key winding |
| Unit | N m | N m (total turning effect) |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Write the formula for moment of force with correct unit.
• Why is a door handle placed at the edge farthest from hinges?
• When is the moment of a force exactly zero?
• What is a couple? Why does the body still rotate if net force is zero?
• 2.5 N at 40 cm from nut — find moment in N m.

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• N m for moment ≠ joule for work — different physical quantities.
• Force through axis gives **zero** moment — NOT maximum (statement III trap).
• Always convert cm → m: 40 cm = 0.4 m, not 40.
• Use **perpendicular** distance, not any slant distance.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s3-sec_01 — force causes translational or rotational effects; phy-s3-sec_14 — numerical moment calculations; phy-s4-sec_01 — N m vs joule distinction in work chapter.

**From linked questions (13 Qs):**
• Moment of force = force × perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.
• Moment of force = force (N) × perpendicular distance (m), so its unit is newton metre (N m).
• To increase the turning moment without increasing the force, increase the perpendicular distance from the axis: a longer spanner (or a pipe slipped over the handle) multiplies the turning effect of the same applied force.
• Equal, opposite, parallel forces acting at different points form a couple.
• Moment = force × perpendicular distance.
• I and II are correct definitions.
• Two equal, opposite, parallel forces acting at different points form a COUPLE.
• T/F: "The moment of a force depends only on the magnitude of the force." → **false** — False.

**From linked questions (13 Qs):**
• Moment of force = force × perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.
• Moment of force = force (N) × perpendicular distance (m), so its unit is newton metre (N m).
• To increase the turning moment without increasing the force, increase the perpendicular distance from the axis: a longer spanner (or a pipe slipped over the handle) multiplies the turning effect of the same applied force.
• Equal, opposite, parallel forces acting at different points form a couple.
• Moment = force × perpendicular distance.

teacherTip: **M**oment = **M**ultiply force by **d**istance (perpendicular). Longer lever = **L**arger turn. Couple = **C**ancel forces, **C**reate rotation.
examTip: Always convert distance to metres before computing moment (40 cm = 0.4 m) — the classic numerical trap.

subtopic: 3. Thrust and Pressure — Solids, Liquids, Atmosphere
content:
**Executive summary:** **Thrust** = normal force on a surface (N). **Pressure** = thrust ÷ area (Pa = N/m²). Same force on smaller area → higher pressure (knife, nail). **Liquid pressure** P = hρg — depends on depth and density, NOT vessel shape. **Atmospheric pressure** ~10⁵ Pa at sea level; acts equally in all directions; decreases with altitude.

**Must know**
• **Thrust**: force acting **normally (perpendicular)** on a surface; unit N.
• **Pressure** = thrust ÷ area; SI unit **pascal (Pa)** = 1 N/m².
• Small area → large pressure: knife edge, nail tip, needle, high heels.
• Large area → small pressure: broad bag straps, camel's feet, skis, wide foundations, tank tracks, railway sleepers, porter's head pad.
• **Liquid pressure**: **P = h ρ g** (h = depth from free surface, ρ in kg/m³, g ≈ 10 m/s²).
  – Increases with **depth** (dam thicker at base; lowest hole spurts farthest).
  – Increases with **density** (sea water > fresh water at same depth).
  – Does **NOT** depend on shape, width or volume of vessel.
  – At a point, acts **equally in all directions**.
• **~10 m water ≈ 1 atm ≈ 10⁵ Pa**.
• **Atmospheric pressure**: weight of air column; ~10⁵ Pa at sea level; measured by **barometer**.
• Acts **equally in all directions**; decreases with altitude (nose-bleeds, fountain pen leaks in aircraft).
• Straw, syringe, dropper, rubber sucker: 'suction' = atmosphere **pushing** liquid toward low-pressure region.
• Body not crushed: internal fluid/air pressure balances external atmospheric pressure.

**Everyday physics**
Pressing rubber sucker expels air → low pressure inside → atmospheric pressure holds it. Sucking straw lowers internal pressure → atmosphere pushes drink up.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Three Pressure Formulas**
| Type | Formula | Depends on | Does NOT depend on |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solid surface | P = F/A (thrust/area) | Force, contact area | — |
| Liquid at depth | P = hρg | Depth h, density ρ, g | Vessel shape, width, volume |
| Atmospheric | ~10⁵ Pa at sea level | Altitude, air density | Direction (acts all ways) |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Why are knives sharpened but bag straps made broad?
• Write the liquid pressure formula and list what it depends on.
• Does liquid pressure depend on the shape of the container?
• Why does water spurt fastest from the lowest hole in a tank?
• How does a drinking straw actually work — what pushes the liquid up?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• 'Liquid pressure depends on vessel shape' is **FALSE** — most repeated A-R trap.
• Skis **reduce** pressure on snow — statement 'skis increase pressure' is wrong.
• Atmospheric pressure acts in **all directions**, not just downward.
• Fountain pen leaks at altitude: trapped air at ground pressure > lower cabin pressure.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s3-sec_04 — equal depth → equal pressure → liquid seeks own level; phy-s3-sec_14 — numerical P = F/A and P = hρg; phy-s2-sec_01 — density ρ appears in hρg.

**From linked questions (35 Qs):**
• Pressure = thrust ÷ area, where thrust is the force acting normally (perpendicular) on a surface.
• For the same thrust, pressure = thrust/area increases as the contact area decreases.
• The camel's large flat feet increase the area of contact, so the pressure (weight ÷ area) on the soft sand is small and the feet do not sink deep.
• A broad strap increases the area over which the bag's weight acts on the shoulder.
• Liquid pressure P = h × ρ × g depends on depth (h), density (ρ) and gravity (g) — but NOT on the shape, width or total volume of the vessel.
• Pressure in a liquid increases with depth (P = hρg).
• Pressure increases with depth as P = hρg.
• At greater depth the liquid pressure hρg is larger.

**From linked questions (35 Qs):**
• Pressure = thrust ÷ area, where thrust is the force acting normally (perpendicular) on a surface.
• For the same thrust, pressure = thrust/area increases as the contact area decreases.
• The camel's large flat feet increase the area of contact, so the pressure (weight ÷ area) on the soft sand is small and the feet do not sink deep.
• A broad strap increases the area over which the bag's weight acts on the shoulder.
• Liquid pressure P = h × ρ × g depends on depth (h), density (ρ) and gravity (g) — but NOT on the shape, width or total volume of the vessel.
• Pressure in a liquid increases with depth (P = hρg).
• Pressure increases with depth as P = hρg.
• At greater depth the liquid pressure hρg is larger.
teacherTip: **P**ressure = **P**ush per **P**atch (area). Sharp = **S**mall area. Broad = **B**ig area, small pressure. hρg: **h**eight, **ρ** density, **g** gravity.
examTip: 'Liquid pressure depends on vessel shape' is FALSE — the most repeated assertion-reason trick in this chapter.

subtopic: 4. A Liquid Seeks Its Own Level
content:
**Executive summary:** In **interconnected vessels** of any shape or width, a liquid at rest stands at the **same horizontal level** everywhere. Reason: at the connected base, equal depth → equal pressure (P = hρg); unequal levels would create pressure difference → flow until levels equalise.

**Must know**
• Liquid at rest in connected vessels → **same horizontal level** in all arms.
• True regardless of shape, width or number of arms.
• **Why**: same liquid, same depth at connected base → same pressure.
• If one column higher → greater pressure below it → liquid flows until levels match.
• Direct consequence of P = hρg (pressure depends on depth only, not shape).

**Everyday examples**
• Tea at same level in teapot body and spout.
• **Water-level tube** (transparent hose) — masons mark equal heights across a room.
• Overhead tank → water in house pipes rises to reservoir level.
• U-tube with different arm widths → still same level.

**Diagram tip**
Draw vases of different shapes with one horizontal dotted line through all surfaces — the line IS the answer.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Why Levels Equalise**
| If levels were unequal | What happens | Final state |
|---|---|---|
| Left arm higher | Greater pressure at base under left arm | Liquid flows right |
| Right arm higher | Greater pressure under right arm | Liquid flows left |
| Levels equal | Equal pressure at same depth | **Equilibrium — no flow** |
| Different arm widths | Same depth → same hρg | **Same level** (width irrelevant) |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• State the principle 'a liquid seeks its own level'.
• Why is the water level the same in a teapot and its spout?
• Does the width of the vessel arm affect the liquid level?
• How is this principle linked to P = hρg?
• What would happen if one arm of a U-tube were higher than the other?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Wider arm does NOT hold higher level — shape and width are irrelevant.
• This is NOT about atmospheric pressure alone — it is about **liquid pressure at depth**.
• Connected vessels at rest — levels are same, not different (T/F trap).

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s3-sec_03 — P = hρg and independence from vessel shape; phy-s3-sec_14 — depth h measured from free surface; phy-s2-sec_03 — liquid behaviour and density.

**From linked questions (3 Qs):**
• A liquid seeks its own level: at the same depth the pressure must be equal, so the free surfaces in both arms settle at the same horizontal level regardless of the width or shape of the arms.
• If the levels differed, the pressure at the connected base would differ between arms and liquid would flow until the levels equalised.
• T/F: "A liquid kept in interconnected vessels of different shapes stands at different heights in each vessel." → **false** — False.

**From linked questions (3 Qs):**
• A liquid seeks its own level: at the same depth the pressure must be equal, so the free surfaces in both arms settle at the same horizontal level regardless of the width or shape of the arms.
• If the levels differed, the pressure at the connected base would differ between arms and liquid would flow until the levels equalised.
• T/F: "A liquid kept in interconnected vessels of different shapes stands at different heights in each vessel." → **false** — False.
teacherTip: Water finds its own **L**evel like people find the **L**owest common ground — equal depth = equal pressure = peace (no flow).
examTip: Draw the classic vases-of-different-shapes diagram with one dotted horizontal line through all surfaces — the line IS the answer.

content:
**Executive summary:** Three formulas: **Moment = F × d** (N m, d in m perpendicular), **Pressure = F ÷ A** (Pa, A in m²), **Liquid P = hρg** (h from free surface). Convert cm→m and cm²→m² (÷10,000) before substituting. Sanity check: 10 m water ≈ 10⁵ Pa ≈ 1 atm.

**Must know**
• Moment = F × d; d = **perpendicular distance in metres**.
• Pressure = F ÷ A; A in **m²** (1 m² = 10,000 cm²).
• Liquid pressure = h ρ g; h in m, ρ in kg/m³, g ≈ 10 m/s².
• **Worked patterns**:
  – 2.5 N at 40 cm → 2.5 × 0.4 = **1 N m**
  – 400 N on 0.5 m² → 400 ÷ 0.5 = **800 Pa**
  – 10 m water: 10 × 1000 × 10 = **100,000 Pa = 10⁵ Pa**
• Depth h measured from **free surface** down to the point.
• Dividing by fraction < 1 gives **larger** answer.

**Checklist**
cm → m. cm² → m² (÷10,000). Perpendicular distance only. Sanity-check with 10 m ≈ 1 atm.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Formula Quick Reference**
| Quantity | Formula | SI unit | Common trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moment | F × d⊥ | N m | Using cm instead of m |
| Pressure (solid) | F ÷ A | Pa (N/m²) | Forgetting cm² → m² conversion |
| Pressure (liquid) | hρg | Pa | Measuring h from bottom not surface |
| Check value | 10 m water | ≈ 10⁵ Pa ≈ 1 atm | Sanity-check all hρg answers |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Find moment: 2.5 N at 40 cm from pivot.
• Find pressure: 400 N thrust on 0.5 m² area.
• Find liquid pressure at 10 m depth in water (ρ=1000, g=10).
• Why does dividing 400 by 0.5 give 800, not 200?
• How do you convert 50 cm² to m²?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• cm² to m²: divide by 10,000 — most-missed step in pressure problems.
• Absurdly large/small answer → check unit conversion first.
• Moment unit is N m — never write joule for turning effect.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s3-sec_02 — moment formula and perpendicular distance; phy-s3-sec_03 — thrust, pressure and hρg definitions; phy-s2-sec_01 — density ρ in liquid pressure formula.

**From linked questions (3 Qs):**
• Convert distance to metres: 40 cm = 0.
• Pressure = thrust ÷ area = 400 N ÷ 0.
• P = hρg = 10 m × 1000 kg/m³ × 10 m/s² = 100,000 Pa (= 10⁵ Pa, about equal to one atmosphere).

**From linked questions (3 Qs):**
• Convert distance to metres: 40 cm = 0.
• Pressure = thrust ÷ area = 400 N ÷ 0.
• P = hρg = 10 m × 1000 kg/m³ × 10 m/s² = 100,000 Pa (= 10⁵ Pa, about equal to one atmosphere).
teacherTip: **M**etres for moment distance. **M**etres squared for pressure area. **h** from the **t**op (free surface). 10-5-1: **10** m water = **10⁵** Pa = **1** atm.
examTip: Memorise '10 m of water ≈ 1 atm ≈ 10⁵ Pa' — it lets you sanity-check every hρg answer instantly.

content:
**Executive summary:** **Work** is done when a force moves a body: W = force × displacement along the force (joule). TWO conditions required: force acts AND displacement along force. Zero work: pushing a wall, coolie standing still, carrying bag horizontally (force ⊥ displacement). **Energy** = capacity to do work; also in joules. Work and energy are **scalars**.

**Must know**
• **Work** = force × displacement in direction of force; SI unit **joule (J)**; 1 J = 1 N × 1 m.
• **Two conditions**: (1) force acts, (2) displacement along (a component of) force.
• **Zero-work cases**:
  – Pushing rigid wall (no displacement).
  – Coolie standing still with load (no displacement).
  – Carrying bag on level ground — supporting force vertical, displacement horizontal → perpendicular → **zero work** by that force.
• 'Feeling tired' ≠ physics work — muscles use energy internally but no work on load if no displacement.
• **Energy** = capacity to do work; unit **joule**.
• Examples: moving hammer (KE), stored water (PE), stretched bow (elastic PE), fuel (chemical).
• Work and energy are **scalar** quantities (magnitude only, no direction).
• **N m** can dimensionally equal joule, but joule reserved for **work/energy**; N m for **moment**.

**Key distinction**
Physics work needs displacement. Biological effort without movement = zero work on the object.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Work Done vs Zero Work**
| Situation | Force? | Displacement along force? | Work done |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lifting a book upward | Yes (upward) | Yes (upward) | **Positive** (mgh) |
| Pushing stationary wall | Yes | No | **Zero** |
| Coolie standing with load | Yes (upward) | No | **Zero** |
| Carrying bag horizontally | Yes (upward) | No (horizontal path) | **Zero** by lifting force |
| Hammer driving nail | Yes | Yes | **Positive** |
| Crane lifting car | Yes | Yes | **Positive** |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What two conditions must be satisfied for work to be done?
• Why does a coolie holding a load do zero work on it?
• Why is work zero when carrying a bag on level ground?
• What is the SI unit of work and energy?
• Are work and energy scalar or vector quantities?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Work is **scalar** — statement 'work is a vector' is false.
• Force without displacement → W = 0, regardless of effort or tiredness.
• N m = joule dimensionally, but moment uses N m and work uses joule — different quantities.
• Before 'how much work?', ask: Is there displacement along the force?

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s4-sec_03 — work done against gravity = mgh = gain in PE; phy-s4-sec_04 — power = work/time; phy-s4-sec_13 — numerical W = Fd; phy-s3-sec_02 — N m vs joule distinction.

**From linked questions (15 Qs):**
• Work = force × displacement in the direction of the force.
• Work and energy are both measured in joules (J).
• Work is done only by the component of force ALONG the displacement.
• Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv².
• The raised, swinging hammer has kinetic energy.
• Work requires both force AND displacement.
• Work = force × displacement; with zero displacement the work is zero no matter how large the force.
• Both have the dimensional form force × distance, but the distances play different roles (along the force vs perpendicular to it).

**From linked questions (15 Qs):**
• Work = force × displacement in the direction of the force.
• Work and energy are both measured in joules (J).
• Work is done only by the component of force ALONG the displacement.
• Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv².
• The raised, swinging hammer has kinetic energy.
• Work requires both force AND displacement.
• Work = force × displacement; with zero displacement the work is zero no matter how large the force.
• Both have the dimensional form force × distance, but the distances play different roles (along the force vs perpendicular to it).
teacherTip: **W**ork needs **W**alk (displacement). No move = no work. Carrying horizontally: force ↑, path →, work = 0. Tired ≠ work done.
examTip: Before answering 'how much work?', ask: Is there a force? Did the point of application move along the force? If either is no, W = 0.

content:
**Executive summary:** **KE** = ½mv² (motion; doubling speed → 4× KE). **PE** = mgh (height) or elastic (shape). Mechanical energy interconverts: falling body PE→KE; pendulum oscillates PE↔KE; total PE+KE constant until friction converts to heat. **Law of conservation of energy**: energy never created or destroyed — only transformed.

**Must know**
• **KE** = ½ m v² — energy of motion; doubling speed → **4× KE** (v squared).
• **Gravitational PE** = m g h — proportional to height (double height → double PE).
• **Elastic PE** — stored in changed shape: stretched bow/rubber, compressed spring, wound clock/toy spring.
• **Interconversion**: falling body PE→KE; at half height KE = PE = half initial PE; at ground all KE.
• Ball thrown up: KE→PE; at top KE=0, PE maximum.
• **Pendulum**: extremes → all PE; lowest point → all KE; total constant until friction stops it.
• Friction/air resistance convert mechanical energy to **heat** — energy not destroyed.
• **Conservation**: energy neither created nor destroyed — only changes form.
• **Energy chains**: torch (chemical→electrical→light+heat); hydroelectric (PE→KE→mechanical→electrical); microphone (sound→electrical); loudspeaker (electrical→sound); solar cell (light→electrical); fan (electrical→kinetic).
• Power station does NOT create energy — it **converts** forms.

**Scaling laws**
KE ∝ v² (square). PE ∝ h (linear). Do not mix them.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — KE vs Gravitational PE**
| Criterion | Kinetic energy (KE) | Gravitational PE |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | ½mv² | mgh |
| Depends on | Mass and **speed** | Mass, g, **height** |
| Doubling speed/height | Speed ×2 → KE **×4** | Height ×2 → PE **×2** |
| Zero when | Body at rest (v=0) | At reference level (h=0) |
| Examples | Moving car, flowing water, wind | Water in dam, raised hammer |
| At pendulum extreme | Minimum (0) | **Maximum** |
| At pendulum mean position | **Maximum** | Minimum |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Write KE and PE formulas. What happens to KE if speed doubles?
• At what point of a pendulum swing is KE maximum? PE maximum?
• Describe energy changes when a ball is thrown vertically upward.
• State the law of conservation of energy.
• Why does a pendulum eventually stop? Is energy destroyed?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Doubling speed → 4× KE, NOT 2× (statement II trap in MCQs).
• Compressed spring has **elastic PE**, not KE.
• Pendulum stopping does NOT violate conservation — energy becomes heat.
• At half fall height: KE = PE = ½ initial PE (Olympiad favourite).

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s4-sec_01 — energy as capacity to do work; phy-s4-sec_04 — power is rate of energy conversion; phy-s4-sec_13 — numerical KE, PE calculations; phy-s1-sec_04 — latent heat is energy at constant temperature (different from KE).

• Stored water at a height has gravitational potential energy (PE = mgh).
• Energy stored in a body due to a change in its shape or configuration (stretching, compressing, bending, winding) is elastic potential energy.
• As the mango falls, its PE (mgh) steadily converts into KE.
• At half the height, the ball has lost half its potential energy (PE ∝ h).
• At the extremes the bob momentarily stops (KE = 0, PE maximum).
• Energy can only be transformed between forms (e.
• The cell stores chemical energy, which becomes electrical energy when the circuit is closed; the bulb/LED converts electrical energy into light (with some heat).

content:
**Executive summary:** **Power** = work ÷ time = rate of doing work or converting energy. SI unit **watt (W)**; 1 W = 1 J/s. 1 kW = 1000 W. Energy (joule) answers 'how much'; power (watt) answers 'how fast'. Same work in less time = more power. A powerful machine need not do more total work.

**Must know**
• **Power** = work ÷ time; SI unit **watt (W)**; 1 W = 1 J/s.
• Bigger units: 1 kW = 1000 W; 1 MW = 10⁶ W.
• **Energy** (J): HOW MUCH work can be done.
• **Power** (W): HOW FAST work is done.
• Two boys, same climb → **equal work**; faster boy → **more power**.
• Powerful machine ≠ more total work — weaker machine running longer can do more total work.
• **Quick patterns**:
  – 600 J in 20 s → P = 30 W.
  – 40 kg girl, 5 m in 10 s: W = mgh = 2000 J; P = 200 W.
• If question gives **time**, it is likely a **power** question.
• Horsepower (hp ≈ 746 W) used in motors; ICSE answers in watts.

**Distinction**
Work depends on height climbed; power additionally depends on how quickly you climb.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Energy vs Power**
| Criterion | Energy | Power |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | How **much**? | How **fast**? |
| Formula | Work done (W = Fd or mgh) | W ÷ t |
| SI unit | Joule (J) | Watt (W) = J/s |
| Larger units | kJ, MJ | kW, MW |
| Same climb, different times | Equal energy/work | Faster → greater power |
| Machine running longer | More total energy converted | May have lower power rating |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Define power and state its SI unit.
• Two boys climb same stairs — one in 20 s, one in 40 s. Compare work and power.
• Is a powerful machine guaranteed to do more total work?
• Convert: 600 J in 20 s → power in watts.
• How is power related to energy?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Watt is unit of **power**, NOT energy (common T/F trap).
• Power and energy are **different** quantities — related but not identical.
• 'Powerful machine does more total work' is **false** — power is rate, not total amount.
• Question with time → think power first.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s4-sec_01 — work is the numerator in power; phy-s4-sec_03 — power is rate of energy conversion; phy-s4-sec_13 — combined numericals (mgh then ÷ t).

**From linked questions (10 Qs):**
• Power = work done ÷ time taken; SI unit watt (1 W = 1 J/s).
• Work done = weight × height gained, which is the same for both.
• A is FALSE: a powerful machine works FASTER, but a weaker machine running longer can do more total work.
• I and II are correct.
• T/F: "The watt is the SI unit of energy." → **false** — False.
• T/F: "Two machines doing the same work in different times have different powers." → **true** — True.
• Fill: **power** — Power = work ÷ time; SI unit watt (W).
• Fill: **1000** — 1 kW = 1000 W.

teacherTip: **P**ower = **P**er second. More watts = faster job. Same stairs, same work — sprinter has more power. Time in question → think **P**ower.
examTip: If a question gives a TIME, it is a power question; if it does not, it is a work/energy question — sort first, then pick the formula.

content:
**Executive summary:** Four formulas: **W = Fd**, **PE = mgh**, **KE = ½mv²** (square v first!), **P = W/t**. Use vertical height only for mgh (not staircase length). Units: kg, m, s, m/s. Work against gravity = mgh regardless of path.

**Must know**
• W = F × d (J)
• PE = m g h (g = 10 m/s² unless stated)
• KE = ½ m v² — **square speed FIRST**, then multiply
• P = W ÷ t (W)
• **Worked patterns**:
  – 50 N, 4 m → W = **200 J**
  – 2 kg book, 1.5 m shelf → PE = 2×10×1.5 = **30 J**
  – 0.5 kg ball, 4 m/s → KE = ½×0.5×16 = **4 J**
  – 600 J in 20 s → P = **30 W**
  – 40 kg, 5 m, 10 s → W = 2000 J, P = **200 W**
• Staircase: use **vertical height** only, never slant length.
• Work against gravity = mgh — path independent.

**Checklist**
kg, m, s, m/s. Square v before multiplying in KE. Energy and work share joule; power alone is watt.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Formula & Unit Checklist**
| Quantity | Formula | SI unit | Trap to avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Work | F × d | J (N·m) | Force ⊥ displacement → W=0 |
| Gravitational PE | mgh | J | Using slant length instead of h |
| Kinetic energy | ½mv² | J | Forgetting to square v first |
| Power | W ÷ t | W (J/s) | Confusing W (work) with W (watt) |
| 1 joule | 1 N × 1 m | — | — |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• 50 N moves a box 4 m — find work.
• 2 kg book raised 1.5 m — find PE (g=10).
• 0.5 kg ball at 4 m/s — find KE.
• 40 kg person climbs 5 m in 10 s — find work and power.
• Why use vertical height, not staircase length, in mgh?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Two classic traps: forget to square v in KE; use staircase length instead of vertical h.
• Write formula line first even in MCQ rough work.
• 4² = 16, then ½ × 0.5 × 16 = 4 J — show squaring step.

**🔗 Links:** Connects to phy-s4-sec_01 — work definition and conditions; phy-s4-sec_03 — KE/PE formulas and conservation; phy-s4-sec_04 — power = work/time combined problems.

**From linked questions (5 Qs):**
• W = F × d = 50 N × 4 m = 200 J.
• PE = mgh = 2 kg × 10 m/s² × 1.
• Power = work ÷ time = 600 J ÷ 20 s = 30 W (30 joules of work every second).
• Correct: **4 J** — KE = ½mv² = ½ × 0.
• (a) Work done = gain in PE = mgh = 40 × 10 × 5 = 2000 J.

teacherTip: **S**quare speed **S**econd in KE. **H**eight = **H**ow high vertically (not slant). Formula first, numbers second, unit last — **FNU**.
examTip: Write the formula line first even in MCQ rough work — substituting into a written formula almost eliminates careless errors.
